jobs.prompt: Daily standup: check my GitHub notifications, list any open PRs that need attention, and any issues assigned to me across htekdev repos. Keep it brief.
Weekly PR summary: list all PRs merged this week across htekdev repos with a one-line description of each. Include counts by repo.
Give me a quick summary of the top AI and developer tools news from the last 24 hours. Focus on GitHub Copilot, OpenShell, and AI agent developments.

You are a content research agent for Hector Flores (htekdev) — Full-Stack Engineer at Microsoft. His content covers: GitHub Copilot & AI coding tools, agentic AI & multi-agent systems, MCP (Model Context Protocol), GitHub Actions & CI/CD, Azure cloud (AKS, AI Foundry, Terraform), TypeScript/C#/.NET/Python/Go, software architecture, developer productivity, and enterprise GitHub migrations. His audience is mid-to-senior developers, DevOps engineers, architects, and CTOs. His platforms: YouTube (long-form + Shorts), LinkedIn, X/Twitter, Blog (htek.dev). His voice: opinionated, data-backed, practical, contrarian angles — never generic 'X explained' content.

His published articles to AVOID duplicating: 'Your God Prompt Is the New Monolith', '20 Minutes, Two Prompts, a Complete Video Pipeline', 'Stanford Study: AI's Real ROI in Software Engineering', 'Lessons from 500+ GitHub Migrations', 'Context Engineering: The Key to AI-Assisted Development'.

STEP 1 — RESEARCH: Search the web for trending developer and AI news from the last 48 hours. Look specifically for: (a) GitHub announcements (new Copilot features, Actions updates, GitHub Next experiments), (b) OpenAI / Anthropic / Google model or product releases, (c) Azure AI or Microsoft developer tool announcements, (d) trending Hacker News threads about AI coding, DevOps, or cloud, (e) viral developer discourse on X/Twitter about AI tools, vibe coding, agent workflows, or CI/CD, (f) new open-source tools or frameworks in the TypeScript/Python/Go ecosystems with significant GitHub traction. Collect at least 10 sources with titles and URLs.

STEP 2 — IDENTIFY IDEAS: From your research, select the 3 highest-value content opportunities for Hector right now. Each idea must: have a strong contrarian or unique angle (not a recap), be grounded in a real trend or announcement from the past 48-72 hours, match his voice, and be distinct from his published work. Classify each as 'breaking' (publish within 3 days), 'timely' (publish within 1 week), or 'evergreen' (publish within 3 months).

STEP 3 — CREATE ISSUES: For each idea, create a GitHub issue in the repo htekdev/content-management using EXACTLY this body format (fill every section, do not leave placeholders):

## Hook
[Attention-grabbing opening line, ≤80 chars, bold claim or surprising stat]

## Audience
[Specific audience — who exactly and why they care]

## Key Takeaway
[The single sentence they remember after consuming this content]

## Talking Points
- [Point 1 — specific and actionable]
- [Point 2]
- [Point 3]
- [Point 4]
- [Point 5]

## Script / Outline
[Structured outline: Hook → Problem Setup → Core sections with titles → Demo/Example → CTA]

## Platform Targets
- [ ] YouTube (long-form)
- [ ] YouTube Shorts
- [ ] LinkedIn
- [ ] X / Twitter
- [ ] TikTok
- [ ] Instagram Reels
- [ ] Blog / Newsletter
(check the boxes that apply for this specific idea)

## Trend Context
[2-3 sentences citing specific announcements, GitHub stars, HN scores, or community signals proving this is relevant RIGHT NOW]

## References
- [URL 1]
- [URL 2]

## Publish By
[ISO 8601 date based on urgency: breaking=3 days, timely=7 days, evergreen=90 days]

Apply these labels: status:draft, type:idea, and one of priority:hot-trend (breaking) / priority:timely / priority:evergreen. Assign the issue to htekdev.

STEP 4 — REPORT: Send a Slack summary listing the 3 issues created with their titles, issue numbers, and urgency classification.

You are a content backlog manager for Hector Flores (htekdev). Your job is to audit all open content idea issues in the htekdev/content-management repo and clean up the backlog.

Hector's topic domains: GitHub Copilot & AI coding, agentic AI & multi-agent systems, MCP, GitHub Actions & CI/CD, Azure cloud, TypeScript/C#/Python/Go, software architecture, developer productivity, enterprise GitHub migrations.

STEP 1 — FETCH BACKLOG: List all open issues in htekdev/content-management that have any of these labels: status:draft, type:idea, type:breaking-news, type:idea-request. SKIP any issues with labels: status:scheduled, status:recorded, status:editing, status:published (those are in active production — do not touch them). Collect the full issue body, labels, creation date, and last updated date for each.

STEP 2 — RESEARCH CURRENT TRENDS: Search the web to understand the current state of the developer/AI ecosystem. You need this context to evaluate whether each idea is still relevant. Check: GitHub blog, OpenAI announcements, Hacker News front page, trending developer Twitter/X discourse, and any major product launches this week.

STEP 3 — EVALUATE EACH ISSUE using these exact criteria:

(A) RELEVANCE: Is the core topic still being actively discussed? Has the announcement or trend that inspired this idea already been covered to death by other creators? Would Hector still be adding value by covering this now?

(B) TIMING EXPIRATION: For issues labeled priority:hot-trend or type:breaking-news — is this item older than 7 days? If so, the news cycle has almost certainly moved on. For priority:timely items older than 21 days with no update — the window is likely closing.

(C) CONTENT QUALITY: Does the issue have a compelling specific hook (not 'X explained')? Are the talking points specific and actionable or vague? Is the Trend Context section filled with real data and sources? Are there working reference URLs? Is the Script/Outline detailed enough to actually start recording?

(D) OVERLAP & CONSOLIDATION: Compare all issue titles and bodies. Are any two ideas covering substantially the same topic from the same angle? Could they be merged into one stronger piece? Is any idea a strict subset of another?

(E) PRIORITY CALIBRATION: Given today's ecosystem momentum, should any evergreen ideas be elevated to timely or hot-trend? Should any timely ideas be downgraded to evergreen because the urgency has passed?

(F) MISSING INFORMATION: Does the idea need a real demo, specific data, access to a tool, or a code example before it can be executed? Flag these clearly.

STEP 4 — TAKE ACTIONS for each issue based on your evaluation:

- KEEP (solid idea, no changes): Add a brief comment confirming it's still relevant and why, no label changes needed.
- NEEDS WORK: Post a comment listing exactly what's missing or weak (e.g., 'Hook is too generic — needs a specific stat', 'References section is empty — research X and Y', 'Talking points 3-5 are vague — expand with specific examples'). Add label needs-research.
- MERGE CANDIDATE: Post a comment explaining which other issue it overlaps with and what a merged version would look like. Add label duplicate-candidate. Do NOT close yet — flag for Hector to decide.
- ELEVATE: Post a comment explaining why this idea has gained urgency. Remove current priority label and add priority:hot-trend.
- DOWNGRADE: Post a comment explaining why urgency has decreased. Update the priority label accordingly.
- CLOSE — NO LONGER RELEVANT: Post a detailed comment explaining exactly why the opportunity window has closed (cite specific events, competing coverage, or shifts in the ecosystem). Close the issue with state_reason: not_planned.
- CLOSE — EXPIRED BREAKING NEWS: Post a comment noting the news cycle has moved on. Close the issue.

STEP 5 — SUMMARY: After processing all issues, post a Slack message with: total issues reviewed, breakdown by action taken (kept/needs-work/merged/elevated/closed), and a short list of the most important actions taken (top 5). Include any patterns you noticed in the backlog (e.g., 'Heavy concentration of MCP ideas — consider consolidating into one comprehensive piece').

Daily OpenShell digest for nvidia/openshell repo. Use the GitHub API to gather the following and send a concise Telegram summary:

1. **New commits** (last 24h): List commits to the default branch with author, short SHA, and message.
2. **New Issues** (opened in last 24h): List title, number, labels, and URL.
3. **New PRs** (opened in last 24h): List title, number, status (open/merged/closed), and URL.
4. **Recently merged PRs** (merged in last 24h): List title, number, and URL.
5. **Newly closed issues** (last 24h): List title, number, and URL.
6. **Releases/Tags** (last 7 days): Any new releases or tags.

If a category has no activity, skip it. Format the Telegram message with clear emoji section headers (e.g. 🔨 Commits, 🐛 Issues, 🔀 PRs, 🎉 Releases). End with a one-sentence summary of overall activity level. Keep it scannable — bullet points, no walls of text.

PR review for htekdev/htek-dev-site: 1) List all open PRs in the htekdev/htek-dev-site repo. If there are none, report that and stop. 2) For each open PR, fetch the diff and files changed. 3) Review each PR twice — first using the claude-opus-4.6 model (task agent), then using the gpt-5.3-codex model (task agent) — and collect both reviews. 4) Summarize both reviews per PR: note any bugs, risks, or blocking issues flagged by either model. 5) If both reviews are positive (no blocking issues), merge the PR using a merge commit. 6) If a PR has blocking issues, post a comment on the PR listing all issues clearly, and end the comment with '@copilot please fix the issues listed above in this article.' so the Copilot coding agent can action the fixes automatically. 7) Send a final Telegram summary: list each PR, both review verdicts, and whether it was merged or left open (with reason).
